title: The AI Service Pricing Methodology
subtitle: Stop undercharging. Start pricing what AI work is actually worth.

sections.title: Why AI Agencies Undercharge (And Why It's Not Your Fault)
sections.content:
There's a specific psychological trap that catches nearly every consultant who starts selling AI services. You know how long something takes you to build. You know the tool costs $49/month. You do the math in your head — "this took me 8 hours and the tools cost $100, so $600 feels fair" — and you've just given away ten thousand dollars of value for a few hundred bucks.

The trap has a name: cost-based pricing. It's what we default to when we don't have a better framework. And it's completely wrong for AI work.

Here's why AI services are different: the value is not in the hours. A junior developer can spend 40 hours building something you can build in 4 using AI tools. Does the junior developer's work deserve 10x the price? Of course not. The value is in the outcome delivered, not the time spent delivering it.

There's a second psychological trap layered underneath: imposter syndrome specific to AI novelty. You might catch yourself thinking, 'I just used ChatGPT and Zapier — I didn't actually build anything.' That thought is costing you enormous amounts of money. Your clients do not have the expertise to connect these tools, configure them correctly, train them on their specific context, build the review workflows, handle the edge cases, and ensure the output is actually good. You do. That expertise has value independent of whether you typed the code yourself.

The third trap is fear of the market's reaction. AI is new. Clients don't have reference prices. So you undercharge to remove friction from the sale, not realizing that your low price is itself creating friction — it signals that the work isn't serious. A $500 'AI automation setup' sounds like a side project. A $4,500 'AI workflow implementation' sounds like a business investment.

Knowing these traps exist is the first step to escaping them.

sections.title: The Value Anchoring Method for AI Services
sections.content:
Value anchoring means pricing against what the client currently spends or loses — not against what it costs you to deliver.

Here's the formula:

**Step 1: Calculate the current cost of the problem.**
How much does the client currently spend on the thing you're replacing or improving? Be specific. If you're implementing an AI customer service system, ask: 'How many support tickets do you get per month? How many hours does your team spend on them? What do you pay those people?' A company getting 500 tickets per month, spending 3 minutes per ticket, at $25/hour in labor = $625/month minimum. That doesn't count the manager overhead, the turnover cost, or the customer experience degradation from slow responses.

**Step 2: Quantify the improvement.**
Now estimate what changes with your solution. AI deflects 50% of tickets = $312/month saved on labor alone. But the real number is higher: faster response times improve customer retention, fewer escalations save manager time, consistent answers reduce repeat contacts. A conservative annual value estimate might be $8,000–$15,000. Now your $5,000 setup fee looks like it pays for itself in under a year — which it does.

**Step 3: Price at 30–50% of first-year value.**
This is your pricing floor. Price your service at 30–50% of the first-year value you've calculated. This means the math is obvious to the client: invest $5,000, get $10,000+ back. That's a 2x ROI in year one, and the system keeps running in year two and beyond with minimal additional cost.

**Step 4: Anchor to alternatives.**
Always know what alternatives the client is considering. An outside content agency charges $8,000–$15,000/month for similar output volumes. Your AI-powered content service at $3,500/month is a 55–75% cost reduction. Say that explicitly. A custom software developer would charge $50,000–$150,000 to build what you're implementing with AI tools in 6 weeks for $8,000. Say that too.

Value anchoring shifts the conversation from 'is this too expensive?' to 'when do we start?'

sections.title: Hourly vs. Project vs. Retainer — When Each Makes Sense
sections.content:
Most AI consultants default to hourly pricing because it feels safe. It's not. Here's when each model makes sense and why:

**Hourly Pricing: Use It Rarely**
Hourly pricing is appropriate in two scenarios: (1) when the scope is genuinely unknown and you need a safe way to start, and (2) for small advisory engagements where you're being asked questions more than building things. 

The problem with hourly for AI work: your AI tools make you faster. If you can do something in 3 hours that used to take 15, you've effectively cut your rate by 80% while delivering the same outcome. Hourly pricing penalizes you for being efficient. It also creates client anxiety — every email from them is costing them money, which makes them hesitant to communicate.

If you must do hourly, set a minimum engagement (8–10 hours) and bill in advance. Day rate framing ($800–$1,500/day) is better than hourly framing — it sounds more senior and doesn't invite clients to watch the clock.

**Project Pricing: Your Default for Most Services**
Project pricing is the right model for implementation work with clear deliverables and a defined end state. It rewards your efficiency, protects you against scope creep (with a clear change order policy), and is simpler to sell. The client knows their total investment before signing.

Project pricing requires a solid scoping process. Never price a project without a discovery call where you've asked about: current tools and systems, team size, data availability, integration requirements, timeline pressure, and decision-making process. A project mispriced is a project that loses money or burns the client relationship.

**Retainer Pricing: Your Path to Predictable Revenue**
Retainer pricing is appropriate for ongoing services where the work is continuous — content production, analytics reporting, fractional advisory, managed AI systems. The key to retainer pricing that sticks: define the deliverables inside the retainer clearly. 'Ongoing AI support' is not a retainer product. '4 blog posts, 20 social posts, and 4 email newsletters per month, with a monthly performance review' is a retainer product.

Retainers should have a 3-month minimum commitment, billed monthly in advance. Price retainers at a slight premium to what a project-equivalent would cost — the client is paying for reliability and priority access, not just the deliverables.

**The Hybrid Model: Project + Retainer**
The best engagements often start as projects (setup, implementation) and transition into retainers (ongoing management, optimization, reporting). Build this transition into your proposal from day one. 'Phase 1: Setup and launch — $6,000. Phase 2: Monthly management — $1,500/month.' This is how you build a client base that compounds instead of churning.

sections.title: The Packaging Framework: Good / Better / Best
sections.content:
Offering a single option is a yes/no decision. Offering three options is a choice between three levels of engagement with you — and most clients pick the middle.

Here's how to structure tiers for any AI service:

**The Architecture Rule: Start with Best, Build Down**
Design your premium package first. What is the complete, fully-delivered, no-compromises version of this service? That's your Best tier. Now remove one significant component — maybe the follow-up support period, or the number of items produced, or the depth of integration. That's Better. Remove one more meaningful component. That's Good.

The reason to design top-down: it anchors the conversation at the highest value. When a client sees your Best tier first and then looks at Better and Good, they're comparing down, not looking for reasons the base tier isn't enough.

**The 40% Rule**
Best should be approximately 40% more than Better. Better should be approximately 40% more than Good. This creates natural upgrade logic: for just $400 more you get $X additional value. When the gap is too small, everyone picks Good. When the gap is too large, no one upgrades.

Example for AI Chatbot Development:
- Good: $5,000 — single use case, up to 3 intents, 2 integrations, standard handover
- Better: $8,500 — up to 6 intents, 4 integrations, 30-day monitoring, one revision round
- Best: $13,000 — up to 12 intents, unlimited integrations, 90-day monitoring, analytics dashboard, priority support

**Which Tier Do Clients Pick?**
In a well-structured three-tier offer, approximately 20% pick Good, 55% pick Better, and 25% pick Best. This means your average sale price is significantly above your base price. Without tiers, the average sale price is whatever clients negotiate you down to.

**The Decoy Effect (Advanced)**
If you want to drive more clients toward Best, make Better look like it's almost as good for only slightly less. The proximity makes Best feel like the obvious choice. This is not manipulation — it's helping clients see where the value concentrates.

sections.title: How to Price Discovery Calls and Scoping Sessions
sections.content:
Free discovery calls are appropriate when you're new and building a reputation. Once you have a portfolio and some authority, free discovery calls become a problem: they attract tire-kickers, they undervalue your strategic thinking, and they put you in vendor mode from the first conversation.

The paid discovery model works like this: you offer a 90-minute 'AI Opportunity Assessment' or 'AI Readiness Audit' at $500–$1,500. The deliverable is a written assessment of their highest-value AI opportunities, ranked by ROI and implementation complexity. If they hire you for the implementation, the assessment fee is credited toward the project.

This model benefits everyone: (1) Serious buyers self-select in — anyone unwilling to pay $750 for a strategic assessment was never going to buy a $10,000 implementation. (2) You earn something even when projects don't proceed. (3) The client has a tangible, valuable document before they commit to anything larger.

At the discovery call itself, your job is to understand three things: what they're trying to achieve (business outcome), what's preventing them from achieving it (current constraint), and what it would be worth to solve it (value). The last question is the most important, and most consultants skip it. Ask it directly: 'If we could solve this problem completely in the next 90 days, what would that be worth to your business?' The answer tells you your ceiling.

sections.title: Handling 'Can You Do It Cheaper?'
sections.content:
When a client asks if you can do it cheaper, they are almost never actually asking you to lower your price. They are asking: 'Help me justify this investment internally' or 'Help me feel confident this is worth it.'

The wrong response: immediately dropping your price. This destroys perceived value, signals that your original price was inflated, and sets a precedent that negotiation works.

The right response: acknowledge the concern, then reframe or rescope.

**Option 1: Reframe to value.** 'I understand the number feels significant. Let me show you the math again — based on what you told me about your current support costs, this pays for itself in month 4. At that point, every month after is net positive. Does the math still not work for you, or is it more of a cash flow timing question?'

**Option 2: Rescope, don't discount.** 'I can't reduce the price without reducing what we deliver, and I don't think a partial solution serves you well. What I can do is phase the project — we do Phase 1 for $4,500 this quarter and Phase 2 for $3,000 next quarter. You get the critical 80% now and the optimization layer when you're ready.' This maintains your rate while solving their cash flow problem.

**Option 3: Name what you'd cut.** 'If I take this to $3,500, here's what I'd remove: the 30-day post-launch monitoring, the staff training session, and the custom integration with your CRM. You'd be responsible for those pieces. Is that a trade-off that works for you?' Usually they don't want to take on those pieces themselves — and suddenly the full price makes sense.

The one thing never to do: volunteer a lower price before they ask. Many clients will say yes without negotiating.

sections.title: Raising Rates with Existing Clients
sections.content:
The right time to raise rates is at contract renewal or at the end of a defined project phase — never mid-engagement unless there's been significant scope change. The standard practice is to give 30–60 days notice of a rate change.

The framing that works: 'As we've built this program together, the scope and complexity has grown significantly from where we started. Starting [date], my retainer rate for this level of engagement moves to [new rate]. I want to give you plenty of time to plan for that — and if it's helpful, I'm happy to lock in the current rate for an additional 6-month commitment signed before [date].'

That last sentence is important: you offer them a way to keep the current rate in exchange for a longer commitment. This is good for both parties — you get predictability, they get savings. And it converts a potential churn event into a retention event.

The clients most likely to accept rate increases without friction are the ones where you've been diligent about reporting value. If you've been sending monthly reports showing what the AI systems you built have delivered — time saved, leads generated, tickets deflected — then raising rates feels like a natural reflection of the value they're getting. If you haven't been tracking and communicating results, raising rates feels arbitrary. Track your results. Report them every month. The rate increase conversation practically takes care of itself.
